text.title: VUI Error Handling — Recovery Patterns
text.summary: The four VUI error types, a three-tier progressive recovery sequence, concrete dialogue examples, and antipatterns for voice interface error handling.

list.item: No input: user is silent — microphone open but no speech detected
No match: speech detected but not recognized as any valid intent
Ambiguous: speech matches multiple valid intents with similar confidence
System error: technical failure in ASR, NLU, or backend service

rule.statement: Apply a three-tier escalation for any failed recognition: Tier 1 — simple retry prompt; Tier 2 — retry with example phrases; Tier 3 — offer a different modality (visual interface, human agent). Never loop back to Tier 1 after Tier 2 has failed.
rule.rationale: Repeating the same prompt provides no new information and drives abandonment; each tier must add information or a new path forward to break the failure loop.

example.code:
Tier 1 — No input: "I didn't catch that. What's the city?"
Tier 2 — Still no match: "I'm having trouble understanding. Try saying something like 'New York' or 'Chicago'."
Tier 3 — Second failure: "Let me try another way. I'll show you a list to choose from." [Switch to visual selection interface]
example.note: Tier 3 switches the interaction modality entirely rather than repeating voice prompts a third time.

rule.statement: For ambiguous input, name the top two interpretations explicitly and ask the user to confirm one — do not guess silently.
rule.rationale: Silent resolution of ambiguous input is the most common cause of unexpected VUI behavior; users cannot debug what they cannot see.

example.code: User: "Call Mary" VUI: "Did you mean Mary Smith or Mary Johnson?"

rule.statement: For system errors, acknowledge the failure immediately, apologize without blaming the user, and offer a concrete next action (retry, switch to app, call support).
rule.rationale: Silent failures or vague "something went wrong" messages leave users without a recovery path and damage trust disproportionately to the severity of the error.

example.code: VUI: "Sorry, I'm having trouble connecting right now. You can try again in a moment, or tap here to use the app."

rule.statement: Never phrase an error response as the user's fault. Omit "I didn't understand you" in favor of "I didn't catch that" or "I'm having trouble."
rule.rationale: Blame language increases user anxiety, reduces confidence in the system, and causes abandonment — particularly for non-native speakers and users with speech impediments.

rule.statement: Always provide a visual fallback path — display a list of options, a text input, or a menu — at Tier 3. Voice-only dead-ends must not exist.
rule.rationale: Some users will never succeed with voice recognition in certain contexts (strong accent, noise, speech impediment); a visual path is mandatory to maintain access.

antipattern.description: Repeating the exact same error prompt more than twice without adding new information.
antipattern.reason: Creates a frustrating loop with no path forward; users abandon the interface after the second identical prompt.

antipattern.description: Silently resolving ambiguous input by picking the highest-confidence interpretation without confirmation.
antipattern.reason: Users experience unexpected behavior with no explanation; trust is damaged and errors compound.

antipattern.description: A voice-only interface with no fallback modality at Tier 3.
antipattern.reason: Users who cannot succeed with voice have no recourse; the interface is inaccessible to them.
